title: Recognizing the significance of Sjögren's disease as a serious and systemic autoimmune disease and expressing support for the designation of April 2025 as "Sjögren's Awareness Month".
plain_summary: This bill recognizes Sjogren's disease as a serious illness that affects up to four million Americans, mostly women. The disease causes severe dry mouth and eyes, tiredness, and pain throughout the body. The bill asks for more research to be done on this disease and for doctors to learn more about it so they can help patients better. It also supports making April 2025 a special month to raise awareness about Sjogren's disease.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution recognizes Sjogren's disease as a serious and widespread autoimmune disease. It also expresses support for making April 2025 Sjogren's Awareness Month.

sections.title: Facts About Sjogren's Disease
sections.text: Up to four million adults in the United States are living with Sjogren's disease. Sjogren's is an autoimmune disease that affects the entire body when the immune system attacks healthy tissue. The main symptoms include severe tiredness, extensive dryness throughout the body including eyes and mouth, and pain. Serious complications can include ongoing pain, major organ problems, nerve damage, brain fog, stomach problems, bladder issues, lymphomas, and in babies whose mothers have Sjogren's, heart rhythm problems. Most people are diagnosed between ages forty five and fifty five, but the disease can affect people of all ages. About nine out of ten people with Sjogren's are women. Doctors do not know what causes Sjogren's, and there is no single test to diagnose it. People with Sjogren's often pay high costs for medicines to control inflammation and dryness, relieve joint and muscle pain, reduce acid reflux from lack of saliva, and treat complications from dryness. Many different types of doctors treat Sjogren's patients, including rheumatologists who specialize in autoimmune diseases, dentists, brain doctors, eye doctors, ear nose and throat doctors, stomach doctors, kidney doctors, and lung doctors. Sjogren's is often hard to diagnose because many healthcare providers are not familiar with the disease. More training is needed so patients get accurate and quick diagnoses. The physical and mental effects of Sjogren's can severely impact a person's ability to work and take care of their home. The federal government needs to update how it classifies Sjogren's as a disease. More research is needed to understand what causes the immune system to attack the body in Sjogren's, including how the immune system and nervous system work together. Clinical trials are testing new targeted treatments that address the root cause of Sjogren's. Research is also looking for genes and environmental factors that might help identify who could develop Sjogren's. More research is needed to find new treatments and clear biological markers to help people with Sjogren's manage their symptoms and complications as they get older.

sections.title: What the House of Representatives Resolves
sections.text: The House of Representatives recognizes Sjogren's disease as a serious health issue that causes significant health problems. The House encourages the Secretary of Health and Human Services and heads of other relevant agencies to expand and increase research of Sjogren's disease and identify new research areas through the Office of Autoimmune Disease Research. They should work to better understand what causes Sjogren's disease and identify biological markers that will help group patients, improve diagnosis, manage the disease, and develop targeted treatments. They should better count and regularly track the number of Sjogren's patients, since many people are believed to be undiagnosed or misdiagnosed, especially those who have Sjogren's disease along with another autoimmune disease. They should update information about Sjogren's disease and share it with healthcare providers to increase awareness and accurate knowledge about the disease. They should build awareness of Sjogren's disease among doctors and communities in underserved areas so that patients with Sjogren's disease are diagnosed correctly and get the care they need. The House expresses support for designating April 2025 as Sjogren's Awareness Month.
